title: SQL JOINs
subtitle: The Most Important SQL Concept
description: JOINs combine data from multiple tables using related columns. Mastering JOINs is essential for backend development, data engineering, analytics, and database design.

title: INNER JOIN
subtitle: Only Matching Rows
description: Returns records that exist in both tables. Rows without a matching key are excluded.
sql: SELECT * FROM Users u INNER JOIN Orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

title: LEFT JOIN
subtitle: Everything From the Left Table
description: Returns every row from the left table and matching rows from the right table. If no match exists, NULL values are returned.
sql: SELECT * FROM Users u LEFT JOIN Orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

title: RIGHT JOIN
subtitle: Everything From the Right Table
description: Returns every row from the right table and matching rows from the left table. Missing matches appear as NULL.
sql: SELECT * FROM Users u RIGHT JOIN Orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

title: FULL OUTER JOIN
subtitle: Everything From Both Tables
description: Returns all rows from both tables. Matching rows are merged, while non-matching rows contain NULL values.
sql: SELECT * FROM Users u FULL OUTER JOIN Orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

title: CROSS JOIN
subtitle: Every Combination
description: Produces the Cartesian product of two tables. Every row from the first table is paired with every row from the second table.
sql: SELECT * FROM Users CROSS JOIN Products;

title: SELF JOIN
subtitle: A Table Joins Itself
description: Useful for hierarchical data such as managers, employees, categories, and organizational structures.
sql: SELECT e.name, m.name AS manager FROM Employees e LEFT JOIN Employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;

title: SQL JOIN Best Practices
subtitle: Write Better Queries
description: Always join using indexed keys, select only required columns, avoid SELECT *, use table aliases, understand execution plans, and choose the smallest dataset possible before joining.
